questions.prompt: Which of these statements is most accurate?

choices.text: If a page is indexed, it will eventually rank.
Crawl, index, and serve are three independent decisions — a page has to clear all three to show up in results.
The Google Search algorithm is one big system that makes one decision per page.

questions.explanation: Google Search runs in three sequential stages — crawling, indexing, and serving — and each is a separate yes/no decision. None of them is guaranteed, even if the others succeed. This is the core mental model the rest of the course builds on.

questions.prompt: A page on your site is listed as 'Crawled — currently not indexed' in Search Console. Which stage failed?

choices.text: Stage 1: Crawling — Googlebot never reached the page.
Stage 2: Indexing — Googlebot fetched it but Google chose not to store it.
Stage 3: Serving — the page is indexed but isn't winning queries.
There's no failure — this is normal.

questions.explanation: The status name tells you exactly which stage you're stuck at. 'Crawled — currently not indexed' means Stage 1 (crawl) succeeded but Stage 2 (index) decided not to store the page — almost always a content quality signal, not a technical bug.
